passages.explanation: A large crowd follows Jesus because they've seen him healing people. Near Passover, Jesus goes up a mountain with his disciples and sees the crowd approaching. He asks Philip where they could buy enough bread to feed everyone. John notes that Jesus already knew what he was going to do — the question was a test to see how Philip would respond.

Philip calculates that even eight months' wages wouldn't buy enough bread for everyone to have a small piece. Andrew finds a boy with five barley loaves and two fish but points out the obvious — that's nothing for a crowd this size. Both disciples are thinking in terms of what's humanly possible.

Jesus has the crowd sit down — about five thousand men — gives thanks, and distributes the bread and fish. Everyone eats as much as they want. Afterward, twelve baskets of leftovers are collected. The crowd recognises this as a prophetic sign and tries to seize Jesus to make him king by force. Jesus withdraws alone to the mountain. He rejects political kingship — the crowd wants a leader who provides free food, not the kind of mission he's actually on.

That evening the disciples set out across the sea without Jesus. A strong wind picks up. They see Jesus walking on the water toward the boat and are afraid. He tells them not to be afraid, and once they receive him into the boat, they immediately arrive at their destination. The event is presented without fanfare — John doesn't dwell on the miracle but lets the strangeness of it speak for itself.

The next day, the crowd realises Jesus wasn't in the boat with his disciples and there was no other way across. They take boats to Capernaum looking for him. When they find him, they ask when he arrived — they're confused about how he got there.

Jesus doesn't answer their question. Instead he confronts their motives: they're following him because he fed them, not because they understood the meaning of the sign. He tells them to stop working for food that spoils and start pursuing the food that leads to eternal life — which he will give them.

The crowd asks what they need to do to perform God's works. Jesus gives a surprisingly simple answer: the work God requires is to believe in the one he sent. He's redefining 'work' — it's not about rituals or effort, it's about trust.

The crowd demands a sign to prove he's worth believing in — despite having just been fed miraculously the day before. They bring up Moses providing manna in the wilderness, implying Jesus needs to match or exceed that. Jesus corrects them: Moses didn't give the manna, God did. And God is now offering the true bread from heaven — not physical food, but something that gives life to the entire world. They ask for this bread, still thinking in physical terms.

Jesus makes his claim directly: 'I am the bread of life.' Anyone who comes to him won't hunger, and anyone who believes won't thirst. But he acknowledges they've seen him and still don't believe. He says everyone the Father gives him will come to him, and he won't reject any of them. His purpose is to do the Father's will: lose none of those given to him and raise them up on the last day. Everyone who sees the Son and believes will have eternal life.

The Jewish listeners grumble. Their objection is practical: they know Jesus' parents. He's Joseph's son from Nazareth. How can he claim to have come down from heaven? They can't reconcile his ordinary background with his extraordinary claims.

Jesus tells them to stop complaining. He says no one can come to him unless the Father draws them — belief isn't purely a human decision. He repeats that he is the bread of life and contrasts himself with the manna: their ancestors ate manna and still died, but anyone who eats this bread will live forever. Then he escalates the claim further: the bread he will give is his own flesh, given for the life of the world.

The crowd argues among themselves about how Jesus could possibly give them his flesh to eat. Jesus doubles down rather than softening the language: unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever does has eternal life. His flesh is real food, his blood is real drink, and whoever consumes them lives in him and he in them. Whether this refers to literal communion, spiritual union with Jesus, or both has been debated for centuries — but John records Jesus making no attempt to make the language easier to accept. He said this in the synagogue in Capernaum.

Even many of Jesus' own disciples find this teaching too difficult. Jesus knows they're struggling and asks if this offends them — what would they think if they saw him ascending to where he came from? He clarifies that the Spirit gives life, not physical flesh — his words are spirit and life. But he acknowledges that some of them don't believe, and he has known this from the beginning. He repeats that no one can come to him unless the Father enables it.

Many disciples leave and stop following Jesus. He turns to the twelve and asks if they want to leave too. Peter answers: 'Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life.' It's not a statement of complete understanding — it's a statement that there's nowhere better to go. Jesus responds by noting that he chose all twelve of them, and yet one of them is a devil. John identifies this as a reference to Judas Iscariot, who would later betray him.
